title: Evaluating RAG Systems — Check your understanding

questions.question: You update your chunking strategy and want to know if retrieval improved. What is the fastest and cheapest way to measure this?
options: Ask a frontier model to rate the quality of answers before and after the change
Deploy to 5% of users and compare thumbs-up rates
Run recall@k against a ground-truth eval set — comparing retrieved chunk IDs to labelled relevant chunk IDs requires no model calls
Compare BLEU scores between expected and generated answers
questions.explanation: Retrieval evaluation requires only a ground-truth eval set (query → relevant chunk IDs) and your search function. No model calls. No inference costs. You compare the set of retrieved IDs against the labelled relevant IDs and compute recall@k. This makes it fast, cheap, and appropriate for CI — you can run it on every indexing pipeline change without incurring generation costs.

questions.question: Your RAG system's RAGAS context_precision score is 0.31. What does this indicate?
options: 31% of queries return the correct answer
The retrieved chunks are 31% similar to the query on average
Only 31% of the retrieved chunks were actually used in generating the answer — the system is over-retrieving irrelevant content
The model's answers are 31% faithful to the retrieved context
questions.explanation: Context precision measures what fraction of the retrieved chunks were actually used (or useful) in generating the answer. A score of 0.31 means roughly 7 out of every 10 retrieved chunks are irrelevant noise that the model has to ignore. The fix is to reduce top-k, raise your relevance threshold, or improve retrieval quality — the model is being asked to find a signal in mostly noise.

questions.question: You evaluate your RAG system by checking whether the final answers are accurate. Your accuracy score is 0.88. Later a user reports a confident wrong answer on a query where the correct chunk was not retrieved. Why didn't your evaluation catch this?
options: 0.88 accuracy is too low a threshold — you needed at least 0.95 to catch rare failures
End-to-end accuracy conflates retrieval and generation failures — a model can produce a plausible-sounding wrong answer from a wrong chunk, which reads as correct when reviewed quickly
The user's query was an edge case not representable in an eval set
Answer accuracy evaluation requires more than 50 examples to be reliable
questions.explanation: When you evaluate only the final answer, a model that faithfully synthesises a wrong chunk looks like a success if the answer sounds plausible. This is the central danger: good generation quality masks bad retrieval quality. The fix is to measure retrieval independently (did the right chunk come back?) before evaluating answer accuracy. An 0.88 end-to-end accuracy score hides how often retrieval is failing.

questions.question: You use the same model to both generate RAG answers and judge their faithfulness. What bias does this introduce?
options: The model will always score its own answers as perfectly faithful
Models have self-preference bias — they tend to rate their own outputs more favourably than an independent judge would, inflating faithfulness scores
Using the same model for generation and judging doubles the token cost without adding information
The model cannot access its own outputs for evaluation
questions.explanation: Self-preference bias is well-documented in LLM-as-judge research: models rate their own outputs more favourably than outputs from other models, even when quality is equivalent. For faithfulness evaluation, this means a model judging its own outputs will produce inflated scores, masking real faithfulness failures. Use a different or stronger model as the judge, or average across multiple judge models.

questions.question: Three months after launch, your recall@5 is still 0.89 but users report the system gives wrong answers on questions that should be easy. What production monitoring signal most directly explains this?
options: Retrieval latency p95 has increased — the system is timing out before finding the right chunks
Retrieval miss rate has spiked — users are asking about topics where no relevant chunks exist in the current index
Faithfulness scores have dropped — the model is no longer adhering to the retrieved context
Context precision has dropped — too many chunks are being retrieved per query
questions.explanation: A stable recall@5 means the system still retrieves correctly when relevant chunks exist. 'Easy' questions getting wrong answers suggests the relevant chunks don't exist — the knowledge base has a gap. Retrieval miss rate (fraction of queries where no chunk passes the relevance threshold) directly measures this. A spike in miss rate combined with stable recall@5 indicates the query distribution has shifted and new topics aren't covered by the current index.
